  / a TEXT column" and becomes a CRDT structure you serialize. For a tool where the two
  writers are one agent and one human occasionally editing a file, that's enormous overkill —
  you'd pay CRDT representation costs to solve a 2-writer, low-frequency race.

  git: three-way merge against a common ancestor (diff3). This is the one that actually fits
  you, because git solves exactly your shape: two divergent snapshots plus a known merge
  base. With the ancestor, non-overlapping edits auto-merge silently and only
  genuinely-overlapping hunks become conflicts. The whole "agent rewrote section 3 while the
  human fixed a typo in section 1" case — which is most of your real conflicts — just
  resolves.

  What I'd actually do here (and why it's not a bandaid)

  The key realization: murder already stores almost everything a 3-way merge needs.

  - last_materialized_hash is already your merge-base pointer.
  - plan_revisions already retains historical bodies + content hashes per revision. So the
  content of the base (the revision whose hash == last_materialized_hash) is very likely
  already recoverable from disk. You're storing the ancestor; you just aren't using it.

  So the real fix is a progression, cheapest first:

  1. Stop the wedge (smallest, do regardless). Today mark_plan_sync_state writes only
  file_hash, never advancing last_materialized_hash or body_hash, so db_changed &&
  file_changed stay true forever and nothing — not even re-editing the file — escapes. At
  minimum, conflict must have an exit. This is the bug-shaped part, independent of strategy.
  2. 3-way merge on reconcile (the real fix). When you hit db_changed && file_changed,
  recover the base body from plan_revisions and run a line-level diff3 of base / db_body /
  file_body. Clean merges (the common case) auto-resolve and go straight to synced. This
  leverages assets you already have; it's mostly wiring, not new storage.
  3. Conflict-copy fallback for true overlaps (Dropbox's trick). When diff3 does hit
  overlapping hunks, don't block — either write conflict markers (<<<<<<<) into the file and
  mark a lightweight needs_review, or spill the losing side to <name>.conflict-<ts>.md and
  surface it. Loss-proof, never sticky, and the human resolves it like a git conflict. This
  is where your "have a planning agent resolve" instinct actually belongs — not as the
  detector/merger (that should be deterministic diff3), but only as an optional convenience
  for resolving the residual overlap conflicts a human would otherwise handle.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
❯ 
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ⏵⏵ bypass permissions on (shift+tab to cycle) · esc to interrupt · ↓ to manage

  ● main                                                        ↑/↓ to select · Enter to view
  ◯ general-purpose  Reconcile conflicted plan                         1m 1s · ↓ 23.1k tokens
